title: Scandium Triflate in Organic Synthesis

using a small amount of Sc(OTf)3 in both organic and
aqueous solvents. In addition, Sc(OTf)3 can be recovered
typical Lewis acids such as AlCl3, BF3, SnCl4, etc. While most

Shu¯ Kobayashi was born in 1959 in Tokyo, Japan. He studied chemistry at the University of
Tokyo and received his Ph. D. under the supervision of Professor Teruaki Mukaiyama in 1988.
He became Assistant Professor (1987), Lecturer (1991), and Associate Professor (1992) of
Science University of Tokyo (SUT). In 1998, he moved to the University of Tokyo to become
Professor of Pharmaceutical Sciences. He was Visiting Professor of Universite Louis Pasteur,
Strasbourg (1993), Kyoto University (1995), Nijmegen University (1996), and Philipps-Uni-
versität Marburg (1997). His research interests include development of new synthetic methods
and catalysts, development of new methodologies for library synthesis, aqueous reactions, total
synthesis of biologically important compounds, and organometallic chemistry. He received the
Chemical Society of Japan Award for Young Chemists (1991), Teijin Award in Synthetic Or-
ganic Chemistry, Japan (1992), and Springer Award in Organometallic Chemistry (1997).

Shu¯ Kobayashi was born in 1959 in Tokyo, Japan. He studied chemistry at the University of
Tokyo and received his Ph. D. under the supervision of Professor Teruaki Mukaiyama in 1988.
He became Assistant Professor (1987), Lecturer (1991), and Associate Professor (1992) of
Science University of Tokyo (SUT). In 1998, he moved to the University of Tokyo to become
Professor of Pharmaceutical Sciences. He was Visiting Professor of Universite Louis Pasteur,
Strasbourg (1993), Kyoto University (1995), Nijmegen University (1996), and Philipps-Uni-
versität Marburg (1997). His research interests include development of new synthetic methods
and catalysts, development of new methodologies for library synthesis, aqueous reactions, total
synthesis of biologically important compounds, and organometallic chemistry. He received the
Chemical Society of Japan Award for Young Chemists (1991), Teijin Award in Synthetic Or-
ganic Chemistry, Japan (1992), and Springer Award in Organometallic Chemistry (1997).

solve some severe environmental problems caused by min-

Aldol Reactions Using Polymer-Supported Silyl Enol
Silyl enol ethers are versatile reagents in organic syn-
thesis.[9] They are used as isolable enolate equivalents and
many useful reactions using silyl enol ethers have been de-
veloped.[9] As a new approach to exploit an efficient
method for combinatorial synthesis,[10] silyl enol ethers
were successfully immobilized onto a polymer. Polymer-
supported silyl enol ethers (PSSEEs) were prepared accord-
ing to Scheme 3.[11] In aldol reactions of PSSEEs with alde-
hydes, it was also revealed that Sc(OTf)3 was an efficient
catalyst.[12] An example of the preparation of a 1,3-diol li-
brary using PSSEEs is shown in Scheme 4. In all cases, the
reactions proceeded smoothly to afford the corresponding
1,3-diols in good yields. While 1,3-diols are successfully
cleaved from the support by treatment with LiBH4, it is
also possible to produce β-hydroxy aldehydes or β-hydroxy

procedure is very simple; just mixing an aldehyde, an amine,
and allyltributyltin in the presence of Sc(OTf)3 and SDS in
water, and no homoallylic alcohol (an adduct between an
aldehyde and allyltributyltin) was produced. It was sug-
gested that imine formation from aldehydes and amines was
very fast under these conditions, and that the selective acti-
vation of imines rather than aldehydes was achieved.
It is noteworthy that the use of the recoverable scandium
catalyst and water as the solvent would result in clean and
environmentally friendly systems.

7. Aza Cycloaddition Reactions
The Diels2Alder reactions of imines (aza Diels2Alder
reactions, imino Diels2Alder reactions) are recognized as
being among the most useful synthetic tools for the prep-
The Fries rearrangement of acyloxybenzene or naphtha-

Eur. J. Org. Chem. 1999, 15227
Table 3. Sc(OTf)3-catalyzed Diels2Alder reaction[a]
ether, and a silyl enol ether worked well as dienophiles to
One synthetic problem in the imino Diels2Alder reac-
tions is the stability of imines under the influence of Lewis
acids. It is desirable that the imines activated by Lewis acids
are immediately trapped by dienes or dienophiles. In 1989,
Sisko and Weinreb reported a convenient procedure for the
imino Diels2Alder reaction of an aldehyde, a 1,3-diene,
and N-sulfinyl-p-toluenesulfonamide via N-sulfonylimine
produced in situ, by using a stoichiometric amount of
Sc(OTf)3-catalyzed three-component coupling reactions
of aldehydes, amines, and dienes were examined. In the
presence of 10 mol-% of Sc(OTf)3 and magnesium sulfate,
benzaldehyde was treated with aniline and Danishefsky’s
diene. The desired three-component reaction proceeded
smoothly to afford the corresponding tetrahydropyridine
derivative in an 83% yield (Equation 12).[28b] Under the
same reaction conditions, cyclopentadiene was used instead
of Danishefsky9s diene to afford the corresponding tetra-

Mannich-Type Reactions in Organic Solvents
The Mannich and related reactions provide one of the
most fundamental and useful methods for the synthesis of
β-amino ketones or β-amino esters, leading to β-lactam de-
rivatives.[39] In the classical Mannich routes to β-amino ke-
tones and esters, yields are sometimes low because signifi-
cant side reactions, such as deamination, occur under the

Mannich-Type Reactions Using PSSEE
PSSEEs reacted with imines in the presence of a Lewis
acid to afford β-amino thioesters, which were reduced to
give amino alcohols (Table 4).[11] In this reaction, although
typical Lewis acids such as TiCl4, SnCl4, and BF3·OEt2
gave poor results, a catalytic amount of Sc(OTf)3 or
Hf(OTf)4 gave better results. This process provides a con-
9. Mannich-Type Reactions Using Hydrazones
venient method for the construction of an amino alcohol li-
Acylhydrazones are aldehyde and ketone equivalents as
well as imines. Their stability is much higher than imines
Table 4. Effect of Lewis acids
and indeed acylhydrazones derived from aliphatic aldehydes
are often crystalline and can be stored, although their reac-
tivity as electrophiles is known to be low. Recently, it was
found that in the presence of a catalytic amount of
Sc(OTf)3, benzoylhydrazones reacted with ketene silyl acet-
als to afford the corresponding adducts, β-N9-benzoylhy-
drazino esters, in high yields (Equation 21).[48] Not only
aromatic but also aliphatic, α,β-unsaturated aldehydes, and
glyoxylate benzoylhydrazones worked well. On the other
hand, the catalytic activation of benzoylhydrazones using a
typical Lewis acid such as TiCl4, SnCl4, or BF3·OEt2, etc.
was not effective in this reaction.
In the solid phase, Sc(OTf)3 also effectively catalyzed the
Mannich-type three-component reactions of aldehydes, am-
ines, and PSSEEs to afford polymer-supported β-amino thi-

Asymmetric Aza Diels2Alder Reactions
Sc(OTf)3 also catalyzes acetalization reactions,[63a,63b]
While asymmetric versions of aza Diels2Alder reactions
acylal formation,[63c] β-selective glycosilation reactions with
using chiral auxiliaries were reported, only one example
using a stoichiometric amount of a chiral Lewis acid was
reported.[60] The first example of a catalytic enantioselective
aza Diels2Alder reaction has been reported by using a chi-
ral lanthanide catalyst.[61] A chiral ytterbium or scandium
catalyst, prepared from Yb(OTf)3 or Sc(OTf)3, (R)-BINOL,
and DBU, was effective in enantioselective aza Diels2Alder
reactions. The reaction of N-alkylidene- or N-arylidene-2-
hydroxyaniline with cyclopentadiene proceeded in the pres-
ence of the chiral catalyst and 2,6-di-tert-butyl-4-methyl-
pyridine (DTBMP) to afford the corresponding 8-hydroxy-
quinoline derivatives in good to high yields with good to
excellent diastereo- and enantioselectivities (Equation 23).
